scenario: Aurora Logistics runs a Camunda 8.8 customer support resolution workflow that handles 5000 incidents per day. Agents must be able to perform any subset of triage, escalation, and refund inner activities in any order, repeating some and skipping others depending on the incident profile.
question: Which BPMN construct allows inner activities to be executed multiple times, in any order, or skipped during the same instance?

options.text: An ad-hoc sub-process marked with the tilde character contains the inner activities, which can run in any order, repeat, or be skipped
An embedded subprocess with a single none start event runs each inner activity once in the order defined by the inner sequence flow
A call activity referencing a resolution process runs each inner activity once in the order defined by the called process model
An event subprocess with a non-interrupting start event runs each inner activity once whenever its triggering event arrives

a.text: Correct. Ad-hoc sub-processes carry the tilde marker and let inner elements be activated multiple times, in any order, or skipped entirely, which matches the support flow's requirement.

b.text: Incorrect. Embedded subprocesses require start and end events and follow the inner sequence flow, so they cannot offer unordered, repeatable, or skippable execution of the inner activities.

c.text: Incorrect. Call activities instantiate a separate process from its none start event and run that process to its end, not an ad-hoc selection of inner activities at the call site.

d.text: Incorrect. Event subprocesses are triggered by events inside a scope and run their own flow; they do not offer ad-hoc selection or repeat execution of arbitrary inner activities.

explanation: The ad-hoc sub-process is the BPMN construct designed for non-linear execution of inner elements, drawn with a tilde marker, with at least one activity inside and no start or end events.
